---
title: "GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly"
toc-depth: 3
---

[View on GitHub](https://github.com/pfelix828/gtm-analytics-warehouse){.btn .btn-outline-primary} [Live docs + lineage](https://pfelix828.github.io/gtm-analytics-warehouse/){.btn .btn-outline-primary}

## What This Project Does

Every company's go-to-market data starts out messy. The same customer is entered
twice under different IDs. The same person shows up under three email addresses.
Marketing events arrive keyed on email while the CRM is keyed on a contact ID, so
a naive join quietly counts the same touch five times. Analysts build dashboards
on top of all this and wonder why the numbers never quite tie out.

**Analytics engineering is the job of fixing that once, in one place, so nobody
downstream has to.** This project is a full analytics-engineering warehouse built
with [dbt](https://www.getdbt.com/): it takes six raw, deliberately messy source
tables and turns them into clean, tested, documented tables that a revenue team
can actually trust. It runs end to end on a free local database with one command,
and every model is documented with an interactive lineage graph you can click
through in the [live docs](https://pfelix828.github.io/gtm-analytics-warehouse/).

## Why This Matters in the Real World

A data scientist's model is only as good as the table it's trained on. Most of the
pain in a data team is not the model, it's the layer underneath: the
transformations that turn raw system exports into trustworthy facts and
dimensions. That layer is what analytics engineers own, and it is exactly the work
I did at Atlassian when I reconciled a large CRM dataset and fixed a join that was
double-counting contacts.

This project recreates that discipline from scratch on a clean, public codebase,
so the craft is visible: the layering, the tests, the documentation, and the data
quality logic.

::: {.callout-note}
**A note on the data.** The source tables are *synthetic and generated with a
fixed random seed*, so every run produces identical output and no real customer
data is involved. The dollar figures below are illustrative and exist only to
exercise the models. What is real is the modeling, the tests, and the
data-quality logic. The messiness in the data, duplicate companies, duplicate
contacts, inconsistent formatting, and the email-keyed marketing join, is modeled
on the kinds of problems that actually show up in B2B go-to-market data.
:::

## How It's Structured

dbt projects are built in layers, and each layer has one job. Raw data comes in at
the top, gets cleaned, gets reshaped, and lands as tables that answer business
questions.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["Raw sources (6 tables, ~86k rows)<br/>CRM · marketing · product · web"] --> B["staging<br/>clean, cast, normalize keys"]
    B --> C["intermediate<br/>reconcile accounts · dedup contacts<br/>fix the marketing join · build the funnel"]
    C --> D["marts / core<br/>dim_accounts · dim_contacts<br/>fct_opportunities · fct_product_usage"]
    C --> E["marts / gtm<br/>account health · buying group<br/>lead-scoring features · pipeline funnel"]
    D --> F["semantic layer<br/>bookings · pipeline · win rate"]
    E --> G["consumers<br/>lead-scoring model · GTM dashboard"]
```

## Three Problems It Solves

I built the project around the three problems that actually make GTM data hard.
These are the parts worth reading.

**1. The same company, entered twice.** The raw CRM has duplicate accounts that
share a web domain, and duplicate contacts that share an email. Report on the raw
IDs and you double-count companies and people. The warehouse picks one golden
record per domain, collapses duplicate contacts, and a test fails the build if a
duplicate ever slips through again. In this build, 408 raw account rows resolve to
400 real companies, and 3,035 raw contacts resolve to 2,826 people.

**2. A join that double-counts, caught by a test.** Marketing touches arrive keyed
on email, not contact ID, and the CRM holds duplicate-email contacts. Join them
naively and a single marketing touch matches several duplicate contacts and gets
counted multiple times. The fix is to join against the deduplicated contact set,
and a dedicated test asserts that the number of attributed touches exactly equals
the number of source touches, so the join can never silently break again. This is
the same class of bug as the join fix I worked on at Atlassian.

**3. Cheap rebuilds and point-in-time history.** The product usage table is built
*incrementally*: a full run scans all 50,000 events, but a routine run only
reprocesses the most recent days, so it stays cheap to refresh. And an account
snapshot records a new versioned row whenever a company's size or industry
changes, so the warehouse can answer "what segment was this account in when the
deal closed," not just what it looks like today.

## The Trust Layer: Tests, Docs, and CI

A transformation is only useful if people trust it. This project treats that as a
first-class requirement, not an afterthought:

- **57 data tests** run on every build, from simple "this key is unique and not
  null" checks to custom business rules like "every domain resolves to exactly one
  account."
- **100% documentation coverage**, enforced by a script that fails the build if
  any model or column is left undocumented.
- **Continuous integration** rebuilds the entire warehouse from scratch in a clean
  environment on every push, runs all the tests, checks documentation coverage,
  and confirms the metrics layer still answers a query. The badge on the repo is
  green because the whole thing genuinely reproduces, not because I said so.

## One Tasteful Use of AI

Writing column descriptions is the chore everyone skips, which is why so many data
warehouses are undocumented. The project includes an optional tool that sends a
model's SQL to Claude and asks for first-draft descriptions, written to a review
file rather than merged automatically. Documentation is reviewed, not
auto-generated, and every description in the repo was written and edited by hand.
The audit half of that same tool runs offline with no AI at all and is what gates
the build. It is a small, honest use of AI: speed up the blank-page problem,
without pretending the machine owns the documentation.

## How It Maps to My Work

| This project | The real work at Atlassian |
|---|---|
| Account and contact reconciliation, duplicate detection | Reconciling a large CRM dataset into a clean source of truth |
| The email-join fix and the test that guards it | The join fix that stopped contacts being double-counted |
| Buying-group role coverage per account | ICP and buying-group identification |
| A clean feature table handed to a scoring model | The lead-scoring and propensity work |

## Stack

dbt · DuckDB · MetricFlow · SQL · Python · GitHub Actions
